summary: Yegge shares six new strategies for working with AI coding agents, drawn from his ongoing experience and collaboration with Gene Kim. He argues software now has a sub-one-year shelf life since regenerating code is easier for AIs than modifying it. He emphasizes agent UX design, dedicating 40% of time to code health, knowing when projects are too early for current models, iterating reviews five times for convergence, and managing agent swarms despite merge conflicts. He predicts 2026 will produce 'super-engineers' running 50-100 agents simultaneously, rivaling teams of 50+ developers.
key_points: Software should now be treated as disposable with less than one year shelf life, since AI can regenerate entire codebases faster than it can fix them
Agent UX is an emerging discipline — getting AIs to actually use your tools requires iterative ergonomic tweaks, not just good design on paper
Spend 30-40% of time on code health reviews or invisible technical debt will slow your agents dramatically
The Rule of Five: have agents review their own work 4-5 times before trusting it, as each pass catches things previous passes missed
Agent swarming is extraordinarily powerful but faces a hard merge problem that may never be fully automatable
money_quotes: We are entering a surprising new phase of software development, in which rewriting things is often easier (and smarter) than trying to fix them.
For all the code I write, I now expect to throw it away in about a year, to be replaced by something better.
Naturally, Claude never uses our fuckin' bridge we built together. I've given up even asking.
AI cognition takes a hit every time it crosses a boundary in the code. Every RPC, IPC, FFI call, database call, client/server call, every eval — it gets a little dumber.
I plan for the models to get smarter, by building tools that don't work that well with today's models.
With vibe coding, reading code is pretty much all you do all day. It's hard for most developers.
A new class of 100x (or maybe 1000x) engineer will emerge — people who have figured out how to wield coding agent orchestrators effectively.
Working with AIs is a little messy and nondeterministic. And I think that's here to stay.
key_insight: AI coding agents have fundamentally changed the economics of software, making code disposable and regeneration cheaper than maintenance, but wielding this power effectively requires new disciplines around agent UX, iterative review, code health, and swarm orchestration.
